data.title: Denny's: America's Diner Can't Catch A Break
data.description:
If you caught my earnings preview, chances are you weren't expecting anything spectacular from Denny's (DENN).

And sure enough, the company delivered a double miss.

This former American staple just can't seem to shake the same issues it's been dealing with for nearly two years: sluggish traffic, unit closures, a scattered promo strategy, and shrinking restaurant-level margins.

To be fair, Keke's, its First Watch (FWRG)-style daytime dining concept, is growing fast across Florida and already reaching important markets like Nashville and Dallas.

But let's be real: that momentum isn't nearly enough to make up for everything Denny's is still struggling with.

Denny's shares even staged a pre-market rally—with bulls focusing more on the reiterated guidance—but as soon as the market opened, the shares began to plummet. As I write this, Denny's shares are down just over 5%, inching ever closer to their 52-week lows.

Right now, it's just over 15% lower than when I last looked.

This day wasn't an easy day, to be honest. Portillo's (PTLO) was slammed by the market when it lowered its FY 2025 sales guidance, and First Watch's results were met with apathy, even with traffic growth.

If you've been following my notes, you'll remember I said to watch the guidance and the Q3 proxy closely. That made all the difference in how both were received, much more so than same-store sales growth.

I'll break each one down in detail soon—but not today.

Today it's Denny's turn.

The Broader Story Behind Denny’s Sales Slowdown

California wasn't a good place for restaurants in the second quarter of the year (especially in June).

This was a stark contrast to everything we saw nationally in the same period—especially with full-service restaurants like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), Chili's (EAT), LongHorn Steakhouse (DRI), and even Applebee's (DIN).

I also flagged some softness in Arizona, where Denny's saw traffic drop 5.9% year-over-year in June.

But here's what I may have missed: Texas didn't hold up either.

So while I expected two of Denny's top three markets to be under pressure, it turns out all three were.

But ultimately, what did Denny's same-store sales look like for the quarter?

To put it bluntly, they fell 1.3% year-over-year for the entire system.

This time around, they didn't break things down between company-owned and franchised locations—but since the vast majority of Denny's units are franchised, it's safe to assume most of the trends we're seeing lean in that direction.

From this perspective, there was a sequential improvement of 190 bp compared to Q1 (170 bp system-wide). It's quite clear that February played a role here. In Q2, although June was apparently the weakest month for Denny's (at least in the Western/Southwestern markets), there was no month quite like February.

This is controversial, of course. Some other sources say things improved by June.

Breaking down this Denny's same-store sales result, we get the following picture:

  1. Traffic plummeted 4.3% year-over-year;
  2. Average check per guest grew 3% year-over-year.

Since Denny's doesn't release the average check breakdown very often, we can only make assumptions.

First, it's pretty safe to say that, at this point, they're facing very easy traffic comparisons, and yet it's still declining in the mid-single digits.

Second, BOGO didn't have much of an impact on either traffic (positive) or the average check per guest (negative). To be honest, I could understand it possibly bringing traffic down to something flat year-over-year, but hurting the average check per guest. However, it seems to me that despite the CEO's positive statements, there hasn't been much of a difference in practice.

Third, the average check growth rate is slightly similar to where the industry reached in June (2.9%). If we break this down further, we'll likely arrive at a negative mix and a mid-single-digit year-over-year price increase.

Promotions and Loyalty: Things to Keep an Eye on in Q2

With the BOGO phase out, Denny's needed to run another value promotion to try to generate traffic. This time, the chain adopted the '4 Slams for Under $10' promotion.

As a heavy-eater, let's dig in a little more here. The name says it all—four Slam options, each hitting a different flavor or value angle: Red, White, and Berry Everyday Value Slam, Choconana Everyday Value Slam, Super Slam, and the classic Everyday Value Slam. Prices range from $6.99 to $9.99, and they're available both in-store and for delivery.

Now, some color on the 'value-per-dollar' everyone's talking about:

  1. Through the promotion, you can get discounts on the original price between 20% and 30.4%, with the absolute biggest savings being on the Super Slam ($4 off under the new promotion);
  2. The Choconana Slam, with a 30.4% discount, offers the biggest discount compared to the original price;
  3. The most affordable item remains the Everyday Value Slam. On sale, it would have been $6.99, the entry-level item, and it rivaled the $5 QSR promotions.

It's also interesting to note that this promotion is yet another (of several) attempts to revive traffic by sacrificing restaurant-level margins.

Let me explain.

Consider that the average breakfast ticket for full-service restaurants ranges from $11 to $13 per guest. The average promotional price gives us a promotional price of $8.74, which is a ~30% reduction in the average ticket.

Now, let's put on our speculation hat again and try to guess the impact this will have on Denny's average check growth.

Suppose the average check per Denny's guest is approximately $13. If we estimate that it continues to grow at 3% year-over-year, it could reach $13.40 next year. Assuming again that the promotion has a 20% impact on the chain's sales mix, the new average ticket would be $12.46. That's a mid-single-digit drop year-over-year!

In practice, if there are no upsell levers and/or increased traffic, this could hurt same-store sales.

When it comes to the loyalty program, Denny's is basically taking one step back and two steps forward. If you have a good memory, you might remember Denny's Rewards launched just two years ago, with things like table challenges and birthday rewards already baked in.

But now they're going a step further. This time, the bet is on deeper personalization.

Using data from 5.5 million digital customers, this new rewards program will likely personalize promotions based on guest frequency, purchase patterns, and demographics.

The CEO said she expects it to drive traffic between 50 and 100 bp in the medium to long term, and some sources estimate it will launch as early as H2 FY 2025.

Keep an Eye on Unit-Level Economics Too!

With seven net closures in the quarter, Denny's is targeting net footprint growth next year alone.

Meanwhile, progress is being made. They've already improved AUVs by at least 5% since the closures began, as well as remodeling more than half of their company-owned restaurants and 10% of their franchises (despite the incentive program I mentioned two or three articles ago).

But that's not the best part.

Denny's is slowly handing back previously underperforming (now stabilized) restaurants to franchisees. And that's a big deal.

If you're a shareholder in an asset-light company, your worst nightmare is watching it quietly shift into something else—especially when the world's throwing risks left and right.

But what if I told you that Denny's restaurants still aren't a good investment for franchisees?

In my analysis, I'm disregarding possible fees to standardize comparisons, but you'll be able to see this clearly:

  • When considering AUVs of $1 million (the same as today) and restaurant-level margins of 12.5%, we would obtain a payback of over 5 years, as well as an IRR of only 7.2%;
  • Maintaining the same AUV of $1 million, but now using restaurant-level margins of 15% (the long-term target), we would still achieve a payback period of over 5 years. However, the IRR would increase to 8.2%;
  • Considering the same AUVs, but restaurant-level margins of 11.5% (in line with the margins reported for owned restaurants in Q2 FY 2025), we would obtain an IRR of approximately 6.8%.

This shows us that Denny's needs to go beyond increasing margins, but also scale AUVs to grow its footprint again.

What franchisee would want such meager returns?

Compared to other casual dining chains like Applebee's (14.5% IRR), BJ's Restaurants (17% IRR for existing locations), and First Watch (a strong 29.1% IRR for new locations), Denny's trails the vast majority.

These are just a few issues to address before growing its footprint again.

Taking a Walk Through the Financial Statements

Speaking purely of numbers now, Denny's reported revenue of $117.7 million, growing approximately 1.5% year-over-year.

Most of this contribution came from the twelve company-owned Keke's cafes added to the portfolio since Q2 FY 2024, partially offset by the closure of Denny's franchises.

Remember, when corporate stores open, you get a nice bump in revenue—but when franchised locations shut down, it's the bottom line that takes the hit. Company-owned units bring in more dollars, sure, but you're only keeping about 10% to 25% in margins. Franchised stores? You're pocketing half that—or more—just in royalties, with none of the operational headache.

That's the magic of the asset-light model.

At Denny's, for example, the franchise margin is 50.7% of revenue, while that of company-owned restaurants is 11.5%.

You can see this in practice when EBIT for the quarter fell from $9.1 million to $8.5 million in Q2 FY 2025.

Net income also fell more than 31% year-over-year to $2.4 million. This represented an EPS of $0.05, about $0.02 lower than the same quarter last year.

Note that this EPS drop was partially offset by buybacks. They currently have about 52 million shares outstanding (almost 5% less year-over-year).

And here it's worth delving a little deeper into these buybacks.

Denny's current program is $15 to $20 million in aggregate for FY 2025. If we disregard the $2.7 million spent so far in FY 2025, they are still expected to return somewhere between $12.3 and $17.3 million to shareholders.

In this scenario, assuming an average buyback price of $3.50 to $4.00, they would repurchase between 3 and 5 million shares, reducing outstanding shares to between 47 and 49 million.

The return from the buybacks would range (if everything goes as planned) from 5.7% to 9.6%. That's a lot, but historically, it doesn't offset the stock's depreciation.

Is It Time to Buy Denny's?

For those who don't remember, I have a price target of between $3.50 and $4.50 for Denny's. Now, if the stock price is currently hovering near the lower end of my price target, if it rises to the upper end, we'd have an upside of over 28%.

But is it really that asymmetrical? I don't think so, but let's dig a little deeper.

One positive point is that they reiterated their guidance:

  • 25 to 40 openings;
  • 70 to 90 closures;
  • SG&A between $80 and $85 million;
  • Adjusted EBITDA between $80 and $85 million.

Since the company most closely comparable to Denny's is Dine Brands (both in terms of guest and concept overlap), let's take another look at multiples.

Using an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8x, also in line with Dine Brands, and considering net debt of $416.3 million, 48 million shares outstanding (already considering those buybacks I mentioned), and accounting EBITDA of approximately $75 million, we would obtain a price target of approximately $3.80.

If EBITDA returns to the $80 million level (as we saw in FY 2022), the price target would rise to $4.60.

But what if EBITDA returns to pre-pandemic levels (>$90 million) in FY 2026, what would the price target be? Well, if we consider the same multiple of approximately 8x EV/EBITDA and discount it to present value at a discount rate of approximately 12%, we could find a price target of $5.

Let's organize this here:

  • Scenario I: EBITDA reaches $75 million, which, without adjustments, somewhat corresponds to the guided adjusted EBITDA. Denny's repurchases its shares as directed. In this case, I see Denny's stock trading between $3.80 and $4.00;
  • Scenario II: If you see EBITDA returning to $80 million levels in FY 2025 (remember, this would require Denny's seeing its EBITDA grow almost 27% year over year) and making buybacks, this scenario gives you a price target of just over $4.50, potentially reaching $4.80;
  • Scenario III: EBITDA reaching $90 million in FY 2026 and buybacks still ongoing. Here I see a real possibility of shares reaching $5;
  • Scenario IV: If Denny's reaches $90 million in EBITDA in FY 2026, we would have a price target of $4.50.

Of all the scenarios, I'm sticking with IV, or even a recovery only in FY 2027 (price target of $4).

They're still seeing bleeding units and low AUVs. They'll need at least two years to see a healthier macro scenario, growth in the footprint, and adjust the promotional mix.

I'm sticking with my 'Hold' rating here. The downside looks smaller than the potential upside, so the short setup isn't exactly screaming asymmetry. And let's be honest, a short squeeze could light you up.

I'm keeping my price target in the $3.50 to $4.50 range to

data.title: AMD Is The Company To Buy In The AI Space
data.description:
Introduction

I remember when Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) was trading at around $2 a share back in 2016, when I was building my first computer. Their Ryzen chipset was about to launch, putting computer aficionados on their back as they realized that these chips could offer better performance than Intel Corporation (INTC) chips at a cheaper price. If I had known back then what I know now about investing, I would have surely parked the bus!

AMD is now a global semiconductor that has seen its fair share of ups and downs and has now transitioned into an innovative major player in the computing industry with the stewardship of Dr. Lisa Su. With the company just recently releasing its Q2 earnings and the U.S. government reversing course and approving the sale of AMD’s MI308X GPUs into China, I believe that now is the right time to assess AMD’s future prospects and make a case for a potential undervaluation.

Operation Overview

AMD separates its revenue base into 4 four different segments.

Data center is currently the largest segment and has been a critical growth driver for the company as it focuses on high-performance hardware for servers, AI, and cloud computing. Some core products include the EPYC processors that have become a flagship product line with their high core counts, that are perfect for cloud providers. As well as the Instinct Accelerators, which are AMD's high-performance GPUs designed for AI training and performance computing workloads. This is where AMD directly competes with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). The segment is set to boom even more in the next couple of years as its server CPUs and GPUs should stay in high demand with AMD stating that its total addressable market will stand at $500B by 2029. The segment generated $14.32B in sales for the LTM and has grown tremendously since 2020 when it generated “only” $1.68B in sales.

The client segment is generally what most people think about when they see the AMD branding, as it encompasses the processors in computers. That’s in this segment where Intel and AMD's rivalry has been fierce for decades, with AMD coming back on top in recent years, pushing the rest of the company forward. Key products include the Ryzen family processors that offer excellent performance at competitive prices. The chipsets rely on x86 infrastructure for which AMD is one of two companies to have this instruction set architecture license and has one of the strongest expertise in chip design. Client revenue has grown well to reach $8.99B for the LTM.

Gaming relies on selling GPUS for gaming-spec PCs and custom chips for video game consoles. Key products include the Radeon GPUs that, when I was gaming, were the cheaper option but less performing and more power hungry than Nvidia GPUs. Though they are now way better than back then with their RDNA architecture that allows better cost-scaling and performance, and have been a strong choice for gamers. The segment also produces Semi-Custom SoCs which is a huge and very stable business as it combines both CPU and GPU technology and is at the heart of PlayStation 5s and Xbox Series. The segment recorded sales of $2.79B, down from 2023 as the gaming market cooled down and the cryptocurrency mining bubble burst.

The Embedded segment includes a diverse range of applications, targeting industries such as industrial, healthcare, automotive, etc. The segment was tremendously boosted by the acquisition of Xilinx, which was a strong performer in the adaptive computing segment. The segment sells embedded processors that are older versions of Ryzen and adaptive SoCs and FPGAs, which are perfect for boosting data centers and powering advanced driver-assistance systems in cars, for example. The segment is high margin with very long design and revenue cycles. The segment generated $3.5B for the LTM.

Q2 Earnings

AMD reported an in-line EPS of 48 cents with a beat in terms of revenue at $7.69B. I believe that it was a strong quarter for the company but the stock is currently down over 7% in the session, which I believe is an exaggeration as the market reacts to disappointing data center revenue and concerns about the restart timing of shipments to China. The MI308 ban resulted in an $800MM hit during the second quarter, which impacted the company's AI business revenue. The upside of doing business in China is set to take time to materialize and the OpEx required is creeping higher, limiting the potential earnings that AMD generates in the segment.

Regarding Q3 outlook, AMD is expecting revenue to reach around $8.7B, representing a Y/Y growth of 28%. This would translate to around 54% gross margin, excluding revenue from MI309 shipments to China.

AMD also posted some interesting partnerships to look forward to, in terms of data center and AI infrastructure. One is with HUMAIN, which will deploy 500 megawatts of AI compute capacity and another would be with Dell, which announced that its new Dell AI platform, such as the Dell PowerEdge servers, will use AMD products to maximize performance. Furthermore, AMD is selling ZT Systems’ data center infrastructure for $3B to Sanmina. I believe that it is the right decision as AMD focuses on its core advantages and competencies in its fabless model.

Current Dynamics

Outside of earnings, there are some important points on which I want to touch to grasp AMD’s current presence in the market. As mentioned earlier, AMD has had an advantage as being one of the rare breeds that has an x86 architecture license, which allows both AMD and Intel to build x86 CPUs for Windows-based PCs.

One of the things that has been worrying me and has been at the core of the demise of Intel’s operations has been the rise of ARM based chipsets that use RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing), which is basically simpler and streamlined for a specific set of instructions, translating into lower power consumption and a smaller die size making it ideal for laptops, smartphones and tablets and embedded systems which are all become more prevalent in today’s computing industry. The design is highly scalable, making it suitable for a wide range of applications, and has been the reason why Apple shifted its products to ARM-based processors, cutting a whole revenue segment for Intel. And while I do not believe that Microsoft will rewrite Windows in another architecture spec, it is something to monitor closely.

AMD has also strongly benefited from its tight relationship with Taiwan Semiconductor, as it follows an outsourced manufacturing model, allowing AMD to take the technological lead over Intel, that faced tremendous difficulties with its internal manufacturing. I expect AMD to continue to dominate its rival as it will be hard to grasp how long and if Intel can stage a turnaround.

Regarding the current dynamic between Nvidia and AMD in the AI sphere, Nvidia is clearly dominating, solidifying its strong moat in the sector as it was already the case in the gaming GPU market. But the market needs a strong alternative and that’s where AMD shines through. AMD is considered the company that will emerge as a second source for AI customers and cloud vendors. It is yet to be seen if AMD’s ROCm open-source AI software stack can rival CUDA, but it’s certainly a strong contender as it offers a more flexible alternative that has attracted partners such as META, Oracle and Microsoft.

I also want to touch on one of the best CEO in the tech sphere, Dr. Lisa Su. She took over back in 2014, and since then, Dr. Su has simply done one of the best jobs in pushing AMD forward with a long-term plan that spanned both PC and server markets on the back of its fabless business model. She beat Chipzilla, and I sure would not bet against her and her management team going forward. She is simply an exemplary CEO.

Valuation

AMD is not cheap according to my general standards of companies I invest in, as highlighted by its Seeking Alpha Quant valuation grade of D-. Its P/E Non-GAAP stands at 47.63, close to 108% higher than the sector median while also boasting an EV/EBITDA of 47.64. The company’s P/E has been trading up on the prospects of AI and how the company can capture part of the huge market. I’m not that worried about it, as the prospects certainly have changed since it was the cheap option for PC gamers. Current FCF yield is low, though, at 1.3%.

Regarding growth, AMD has posted some strong top-line growth at 13.71% CAGR over the past 3 years, thanks to the surge of AI-related computing infrastructure needed. Though its net income has decreased, I believe that’s more to do with Macro dynamics instead of the business souring. AMD, like many computing companies, had to navigate the post-pandemic downturn as there were reduced customer orders for PC components and pricing pressure due to the weaker demand. Another reason would be the reduced demand coming from crypto miners that sends prices up due to a supply shortage, which translates into higher margins in the segment for AMD.

I expect growth to accelerate as AMD captures a larger market share in its Data Center and client segments. I believe that AMD can reach 20% annualized Top-line growth through the end of the decade.

Gross margin is relatively strong and has gained over 24 percentage points since 2016 on the back of Dr. Lisa Su’s transformation of the business. I expect this trend to continue as AMD grows its client and data center sales, expanding gross margin closer to 60% by the end of the decade. That’s why I’m not worried about the smaller net margin that AMD has. Though AMD’s return metrics are lagging, I expect them to recover as well on the back of the data center expansion. At the current state of 4.8% ROE, it is simply not enough.

Risks

AMD could and has been impacted by tariffs and U.S. restrictions on AI sales to China, which can hurt the prospects of the company as it limits its growth opportunities. Furthermore, the main driver of share appreciation has been AMD’s position in the AI industry, though Nvidia is the clear leader with not only better products but also better software in the space. Another aspect of hardware specifically designed for AI applications would be the fact that companies such as Amazon and Google could and are designing their processing units to work with their own workloads.

Regarding the PC segment, I already mentioned my worries about the x86 architecture, but diving deeper, AMD is still in a fight with Intel, which could regain its manufacturing lead. This would reduce AMD’s current market share in Windows-based computers that could further decide to switch to ARM-based RISC chipsets or even newer competitors that are now gaining traction in the RISC model, now that it has proven that it can work.

Conclusion

Despite shares currently being expensive, I believe that AMD offers a compelling valuation in both the AI and semiconductor space. AMD has a strong footing in its legacy CPU segment, which generates stable cash flows in order to boost the company's growth in its data center segment, offering higher margins and better return on capital. The current management is one of the best in the tech sector and I will surely bet on them to come up with a way to better rival Nvidia. I rate AMD as a Buy.
